serving.natural_language_portion: One typical hawker serving of fried fish beehoon soup is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of clear broth with deep-fried fish slices, thick rice vermicelli, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Fried fish beehoon soup (no milk) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fried fish beehoon soup (no milk)', CR ID F05060058. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (white, boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried fish beehoon soup (no milk) as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice vermicelli (beehoon), which has a reported GI of approximately 58–67 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The fish and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 38.96) / 100 ≈ 25.3, using a 400 g serving with 9.74 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 306 mg/100 g; estimated 1224 mg per 400 g serving, approaching the daily recommended limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid drinking the soup broth entirely, request reduced fish stock and less added salt, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) is a refined carbohydrate with medium-to-high GI (~65); dietary fibre is low at 0.74 g/100 g, providing insufficient glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller beehoon portion with extra fish and vegetables, and consider substituting with lower-GI noodles such as glass noodles or wholemeal options where available to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 306 mg/100 g raises blood pressure risk; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and increases saturated fat contribution; total fat is moderate at 2.63 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit soup broth consumption to reduce sodium load, request steamed or lightly fried fish to minimise AGE formation, and ensure the dish is consumed as part of a diet rich in vegetables and whole grains.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that may exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium is borderline high; fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request steamed or lightly fried fish to reduce AGE exposure, avoid drinking all the soup broth to limit sodium, and note that the omega-3 content from fish may provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Beehoon Soup (No Milk) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of deep-fried sliced fish pieces, thick white rice vermicelli (beehoon), and vegetables served in a clear, savoury fish broth. Unlike the milky variant, this version uses a transparent stock made from fish bones and ginger, resulting in a lighter, cleaner flavour profile. The fish — typically batang (Spanish mackerel) or snapper — is coated and deep-fried until golden and crispy before being placed in the hot broth, which softens the exterior while retaining a tender interior. Common vegetables include tomato, lettuce, and spring onion. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal. Nutritionally, it provides 70 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (2.82 g), low fat (2.63 g), and carbohydrate primarily from the beehoon (9.74 g/100 g). Sodium is elevated at 306 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, particularly if the broth is consumed in full. The GI is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the white rice vermicelli component. Dietary fibre is low at 0.74 g/100 g. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal. It is not suitable for strict low-sodium or low-GI diets without modification.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060058) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice vermicelli component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which introduces moderate uncertainty for the GI field only.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singapore hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060058, and no equivalent breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.60 g/100 g based on typical deep-fried white fish profile from HPB data context; this value is consistent with the input health_suitability notes referencing ~0.6 g/100 g saturated fat. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice vermicelli (beehoon) as the dominant carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); the composite dish GI has not been directly measured. Input cultural tags ('western', 'vegetarian', 'plant_based') were corrected: this is a Singaporean hawker dish containing fish and is not vegetarian, vegan, or Western cuisine. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 400 g serving size: GL = (65 × 38.96) / 100 ≈ 25.3.
